coredata.dc:title: Room-temperature synthesis of graphene quantum dots via electron-beam irradiation and their application in cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Liang
Li, Weitao
Wu, Bin
Li, Zhen
Pan, Dengyu
Wu, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: We report a room-temperature strategy for the synthesis of single-crystalline fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via electron-beam irradiation. The precursor contained high-activity nitro groups is easy to fusion GQDs with the method. Under optimized conditions (0.02g 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, radiation dose of 400kGy and 10ml 0.5mol/L hydrazine hydrate), the GQDs exhibit highly efficient fluorescence at 475 nm with a quantum yield of 32%. The PL maximum is well corresponded with the excitation wavelength. Moreover, the single-exponential fluorescence lifetime (4.86ns) exhibited the intrinsic PL characteristic. The pH stability in neutral and alkaline solution, solid solubility and storage time stability of GQDs are satisfactory. Their application as a safety fluorescent probe for cell imaging is demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Electron-beam irradiation
Fluorescent probe
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL QYs of the GQDs prepared at different hydrazine hydrate concentration (a), radiation dose (b) and 1,3,6-trinitropyrene quality (c).
Raman (a), XRD (b), FT-IR (c), survey XPS spectrum (d), High-resolution C 1s (e), N 1s (f), and O 1s (g) spectra of GQDs.
TEM image (a), Diameter distribution (b), AFM image (c), Height distribution (d) and Height profile along the line A-B in (e) of the GQDs. Insets in a: HRTEM and FFT image of GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL and PLE spectra of GQDs. (b) Normalized PL spectra of the GQDs at excitation wavelengths varying from 370 to 430nm. (c) Time-resolved PL spectrum of GQDs. Insets in a: Detail UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs varying from 380 to 420nm.

(a) Dependence of PL intensity of GQDs on pH values. (b) Dependence of PL intensity of GQDs before (black line) and after (red line) drying at 80°C. (c) Dependence of PL intensity of GQDs on storage times. Insets: Photographs of GQDs taking under visible and UV lights. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Confocal images and cytotoxicity assessment of MCF7 cells incubated with GQDs: fluorescence image (a), bright-field image (b) and merged image (c) of MCF7 cells excited at 405nm. (d) Cell viability assay of GQDs at the different doses and incubation times.
Z-axis imaging from the top to bottom of the MCF7 cells.
Preparation procedure of GQDs by EBI.

head.ce:title: Room-temperature synthesis of graphene quantum dots via electron-beam irradiation and their application in cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science and Technology, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Applied Radiation Institute, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a room-temperature strategy for the synthesis of single-crystalline fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via electron-beam irradiation. The precursor contained high-activity nitro groups is easy to fusion GQDs with the method. Under optimized conditions (0.02g 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, radiation dose of 400kGy and 10ml 0.5mol/L hydrazine hydrate), the GQDs exhibit highly efficient fluorescence at 475 nm with a quantum yield of 32%. The PL maximum is well corresponded with the excitation wavelength. Moreover, the single-exponential fluorescence lifetime (4.86ns) exhibited the intrinsic PL characteristic. The pH stability in neutral and alkaline solution, solid solubility and storage time stability of GQDs are satisfactory. Their application as a safety fluorescent probe for cell imaging is demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a zero-dimension graphene material, have raised much more attentions in comprehensive applications such as bioimaging [1–5], biosensing [6–8], light emitting diodes [9,10], solar cells [11,12], and photocatalysis [13,14] due to their unique physical and chemical properties. Recent five years, many methods, such as hydrothermal method [15–17], acid oxidation [7,18–21], electrochemical synthesis [22–25], pyrolysis [26–28], and microwave treatment [29–30], were usually used to fabricate GQDs. Among these methods, they can be divided into two types: top-down and bottom-up. The top-down method usually chose graphite [6,15,22–24,29], graphene [1–5,7–9,11–13,17–19], and carbon fiber [10,20] as precursors, then used them to produce GQDs thought chemical cutting and electrochemical treatment. Because the process is uncontrollable and the structure of precursors is amorphous, the structure of GQDs contains much more defects, so the optical characteristic of GQDs is mainly responsible in the UV region. The GQDs with poor optical properties are none-suitable applied for bio-imaging. Meanwhile, the yield of GQDs synthesized by these cutting method is low, just have tens of milligrams or even a few milligrams. Apart from the top-down method, the bottom-up strategy employs small organic molecules as precursors [16,25], then synthesized GQDs by multi-step chemical reactions. The process needs to consume a large number of organic solvent, most of them are toxicity and harmful to the health of human and environment. Besides, the reaction requires expensive catalysts and harsh experimental conditions (high or ultra-low temperature, high pressure). Therefore, the wide applications of GQDs are limited by this complex and high-cost method.

Irradiation techniques have been extensively employed to synthesize various nanomaterials such as metals, oxides, and semiconductors, and graphene sheets [31–36], because they are simple, rapid, mild, and potential for controlling mass production at room temperature. To date, there have been no reports on the application of irradiation techniques for the synthesis of GQDs. Recently, we used nitrated compound of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAHs) as a precursor for synthesizing GQDs [14,21]. Compared with graphite, graphene and other small-molecular organic compound precursors, the nitrated compound of small size PAHs is more reactive, easy to surface functionalization, and conducive to produce the defect-free GQDs.

Here, we report an irradiation strategy for the synthesis of fluorescent GQDs by electron-beam irradiation (EBI) of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in aqueous solutions of hydrazine hydrate. Compared with the previously reported hydrothermal fusion method [14,21] at 200°C, the irradiation fusion can occur at room temperature owing to high electronic energy from the EBI. Despite the low-temperature synthesis, the water-soluble GQDs remain highly fluorescent and ultra-stabilities, suggesting that they can be applied in bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, and other fields.

Scheme 1 outlines the main preparation process of the fluorescent GQDs. A typical preparation process for the synthesis of GQDs via the EBI technology was accomplish as below. A certain amount of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was added into 10ml given the concentration of aqueous hydrazine hydrate under stirring condition. The dispersions were sealed in a plastic bag and irradiated at the different doses under the titanium window of a GJ-2-II Dynamitron electron accelerator. After irradiation, the samples were filtered through a 0.22mm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon product, and further dialyzed in a dialysis bag for two days to remove small unfused molecules. 1-Nitropyrene, urea and citric acid as precursors are used to synthesis the GQDs at the same conditions.

Samples were characterized by and atomic force microscopy (AFM) using a SPM-9600 atomic force microscope, transmission electron microscope (TEM) on a JEOL JEM-2010F electron microscope operating at 200kV, X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) with a Rigaku D/max-2500 using Cu Ka radiation, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy recorded on a Bio-Rad FTIR spectrometer FTS165, and Raman spectroscopy on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with 633nm. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were analyzed on a Hitachi 3100 spectrophotometer and a Hitachi 7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were achieved with an AMICUS electron spectrometer from SHIMADZU using 300W Al Kα radiation. The base pressure was about 3×10−12 bar. The photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of GQDs were determined by comparing the integrated PL intensities (excited at 390nm) and the absorbency values using 9,10-bis(phenyl ethynyl) anthracene in cyclohexane as the reference [37].

MCF7 cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle (DMEM) medium (Gibco, USA) with L-glutamine, penicillin/streptomycin (Gibco, USA), 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (Gibco, USA). Approximately 2×105 MCF7 cells were seeded in culture dishes (diameter: 40mm) and cultured using the same culture medium (2ml per dish) at 37°C under 5% CO2/95% air. All cells were incubated for at least 24h until ∼80% confluence was reached. A GQD aqueous solution was introduced into the cells with a final concentration of ∼20mg/L in the culture medium. The cells were examined under a confocal microscope (Leica TCS SP5) using lasers of 405nm.

Cell viability assays were performed using an MTT assay with 3-(4,5-dimethyl-thiazole-2-yl)-2,5-phenyltetrazolium bromide. The cell viability was expressed as the percentage of viable cells in total cells. NIH-3T3 cells were seeded in 96-well plates (5×103 cells per well) and pre-cultured in medium containing the GQDs at a dose of 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100mg/L for 6–48h. The cell viability of each well was treated with 20μl of MTT solution (5mg/ml) and incubated at 37°C for 4h. 150μl DMSO was added after the medium was removed and the absorbance of each well at 490nm was measured using a Microplate Reader (Thermo, USA).

Fig. 1 shows that the PL quantum yields (QYs) of the GQDs are strongly dependent on preparation conditions such as irradiated dose, hydrazine hydrate concentration and 1,3,6-trinitropyrene quality. The 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (0.02g in 10ml hydrazine hydrate solution) is irradiated at the irradiated dose of 400kGy, the QYs increase rapidly to 32% with increasing the hydrazine hydrate concentration to 0.5mol/L, then a slight decrease during higher hydrazine hydrate concentrations. When 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (0.02g) adds into 10ml 0.5mol/L hydrazine hydrate solution, and the irradiated dose is ranged from 50kGy to 400kGy, the QYs of GQDs also increase markedly from 7.6% at 50kGy to 32% at 400kGy. Compared with the reaction conditions of irradiated dose and hydrazine hydrate concentration, the change of precursor qualities has smaller influence on the QYs of GQDs. Finally, the GQDs solution synthesized under optimized conditions (0.02g 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, radiation dose of 400kGy and 10ml 0.5mol/L hydrazine hydrate) release bright blue light under a UV light irradiation with a yield of 37% in Scheme 1.

To understanding the unusual room-temperature catalyst-free fusion phenomenon, we consider the high activity of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene towards fusion reactions. In a control experiment, commercial 1-nitropyrene (contained one nitro group), urea and citric acid were used to replace the 1,3,6-trinitropyrene for synthesizing the GQDs at the same reaction conditions. Only 1-nitropyrene as precursor can fusion GQDs with low QYs (4.6%) (Figs. S1–S2 in Supplementary section), two other precursors were not produced any products (Fig. S3 in Supplementary section). This contrast indicates that increasing nitro group number in nitrified pyrene derivative enables a dramatic enhancement in the fusion activity. Scheme 2 outlines possible reaction pathways from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene molecules into GQDs in hydrazine hydrate solution. This mechanism involves three key steps: (1) the conversion of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene molecules into water-soluble trihydropyrene monomers via a typical nucleophilic substitution reaction, (2) polycondensation and cyclodehydrogenation reactions between monomers to produce OH-functionalized graphene clusters, and (3) final fusion into large GQDs by adding monomers onto graphene clusters. Because electron-accepting nitro groups induce positive charge at the linked carbon atoms in the 1,3,6-trinitropyrene molecule, hydrazine hydrate, as an electron-donating chemical specie, can attack these positively charged carbon active sites easily, and thus nucleophilic substitution reactions take place under mild conditions.

The GQDs are characterized by Raman, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS in Fig. 2 . The Raman spectrum can exhibit the GQDs quality of the crystalline G band at 1579cm−1 and the disordered D band at 1357cm−1, a large intensity ratio G to D is 1.07. The (002) interlayer spacing of the GQDs is 4.02Å shown in the XRD pattern. In the FT-IR spectrum, stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H are shown at 3200–3400cm−1, the CC, C-OH, and C-N stretching peaks are appeared at 1620, 1064 and 820cm−1, respectively, and the bands at 2800–3000cm−1 are allocated to the C–H vibration peak. The GQDs surface is functionalized by lots of amino and hydroxyl groups, being responsible for their excellent hydrophilic nature. The total XPS spectrum confirms that the GQDs contain three elements of C, N, and O. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum reveals two peaks. The main peak at 284.3eV is CC and the other minor peak at 285.8eV is CN. Only one peak at 398.2eV in the N 1s spectrum is distributed to NH bonding of the GQDs. The peak at 532.2eV is ascribed to the OH bonding in the O 1s spectrum.

The TEM image of the GQDs in Fig. 3 a shows the GQDs are uniformly dispersed in solution with the average diameter of ∼4.58nm (Fig. 3b). The HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 3a) gives evidence for the single-crystalline structure of GQDs. A crystal plane spacing of 0.21nm is fit well with graphene (100) facet. The corresponding FFT image (inset in Fig. 3a) appears the hexagonal carbon network of GQDs. AFM image of GQDs (Fig. 3c–e) indicates their topographic heights are nearly ranged from 0.5nm to 4nm with an average thickness of 1.86nm.

The optical capabilities of the GQDs are obtained in Fig. 4 . Their ABS spectrum (Fig. 4a) exhibits a typical absorption band at ca. 390nm. The optimal maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs are located at 390nm and 475nm (PL, PLE in Fig. 4a), respectively. The PL maximum is independent with excitation wavelength (Fig. 4b). The PL decay curve (Fig. 4c) is characteristic of a single-exponential lifetime (4.86ns). Because the GQDs produced by EBI are mainly applied for cell imaging, the pH stability of GQDs should be tested. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the PL intensity of GQDs are small changed in alkaline and neutral conditions, then decrease continuously under acidic condition, owing to the aggregation of the GQDs observed in adding hydrochloric acid process. The solid solubility of GQDs is shown in Fig. 5b. The GQDs solution is dried at 80°C, then the GQDs solid is dissolved in an equal volume of water. The PL intensity of the two samples are no significant changed, and also emit bright blue fluorescence when irradiated by a UV light. The amino and hydroxyl groups are responsible for the solubility of the GQDs in water. The PL stability of the GQDs is also tested in a long time (Fig. 5c), during increasing the storage time from 1day to 7days, the PL intensity of GQDs is slight changed, illustrated that the storage property of GQDs is stability.

To investigate the prospects of the GQDs as a fluorescent probe for bio-applications, the GQDs are used to image MCF7 cells as the model. Compared with the bright-field imaging of the MCF7 cells (Fig. 6 b), the cytoplasm of MCF7 cells can be brightly imaged by GQDs (Fig. 6a,c). The z-axis cell imaging of MCF7 cells is shown in Fig. 7 , which are the evidence for the GQDs entering into the cytoplasm of the cells. Meanwhile, the cellular toxicity of GQDs was investigated by MTT viability assay. As shown in Fig. 6d, if used GQDs concentration is less than 60mg/L, the GQDs is none-cytotoxicity for cell-imaging even the incubation time increases 48h. When the GQDs concentrations further increase to 80–100mg/L, the GQDs is also none-cytotoxicity under changing the conditions of the incubation time from 6 to 24h. Furthermore, when the incubation time and the GQDs concentration are more than 24h and 60mg/L, respectively, the cell viability becomes below 80%, indicating of low cytotoxicity. Note that due to high QYs of GQDs, the cell imaging tests can carry out at a very low and security GQDs concentration (20mg/L). This result indicates that the GQDs can serve as a low-cytotoxicity fluorescent probe for bioimaging.

In summary, single-crystalline fluorescent GQDs were synthesized at room temperature based on EBI. Under optimized irradiation conditions (0.02g 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, radiation dose of 400kGy and 10ml 0.5mol/L hydrazine hydrate), the PL QYs can be increased to 32%. The GQDs are stability in alkaline and neutral conditions. The GQDs solid is also quickly dissolved in water solution and no change their optical properties. The PL intensity of GQDs is slight changed during increasing the storage time. Their application as a safety fluorescent probe at appropriate doses is demonstrated for bright cell imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21671129), the Shanghai Sailing Program (No. 16YF1404400), the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No. 16ZR1412100), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT13078).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2016.10.022.
